results.possibleIdentity: John E. Morse was an engineer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, primarily employed by the Eastman Kodak Company in Rochester, New York. His technical expertise encompassed electrical, optical, photographic, and mechanical engineering, fields in which he contributed several inventions and professional papers. His work at Kodak focused on photographic film handling mechanisms and display technologies. Notably, he developed and patented apparatus for recording and reproducing copies of images displayed on cathode ray tubes (CRTs) and advanced processes for film cartridge assembly. Morse was a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) program, which maintained a registry of technical professionals to facilitate collaborations with artists. His professional presence is also noted in journals such as 'Information Display,' reflecting his involvement in the broader technical community of Rochester during this period.
